entries.title: Severe wind topples power lines, delays travel amid roller-coaster week of weather in Chicago
entries.summary: <p>Cole Rapp and Joey Cleary were on board a Frontier Airlines flight from Austin, Texas, to Chicago when the strong winds buffeting the city turned their four-hour trip for St. Patrick's Day festivities into a 12-hour adventure.</p><p>"That was probably the most turbulence I've ever felt in my life," said Cleary, 25. </p><p>"It was like a roller coaster," said Rapp, 26, whose hat blew off Friday while speaking to a reporter in Lincoln Park. </p><p>After two more landing attempts, the flight was diverted and wound up 300 miles away in Cincinnati. Rapp and Cleary were eventually told the next flight to Chicago wasn't until 4 p.m.</p><p>They weren't giving up.</p><p>"We booked a rental car, ran into [Cleary's] buddy from high school, packed five people in a car and drove 4½ hours and running on no sleep for St. Paddy's," Rapp said. </p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Cole Rapp (left) and Joey Cleary, who tried to fly into Chicago from Austin, Texas, for St. Patrick’s Day, arrive Friday in Lincoln Park. Turbulence prevented their landing at O’Hare, and after being diverted to Cincinnati, they ended up driving here from Ohio.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Ashlee Rezin/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>The severe wind came after a week of weather that swung from one extreme to the next. Monday's record-setting high of 73 degrees at O'Hare Airport came before chillier temperatures Tuesday. Temperatures then rose again Tuesday night as tornadoes swept the area south of the city <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/weather/2026/03/12/hail-record-size-chicago-illinois-storm-climate-change" >leaving giant hail stones</a> in its wake.</p><p>Friday's gusts, nearing 60 mph, caused disruptions across the city, from power outages to travel delays. There was a brief ground stop at O'Hare, which expired about 7:15 a.m., according to the Federal Aviation Administration. A high wind warning was in effect for the Chicago area until 4 p.m. as forecasters anticipated gusts surpassing 60 mph.</p><p>Train service was also affected by the wind. Metra Union Pacific train No. 312 scheduled to depart from Highland Park was running 20 minutes behind schedule due to an obstruction on the tracks. On the CTA Yellow Line, service was temporarily suspended for the same reason.</p><p>Wind gusts at O'Hare and Midway Airport peaked at 53 and 56 mph, respectively, as of about 5 a.m., the National Weather Service said. Valparaiso Airport in Indiana reported gusts at 63 mph and DuPage Airport reached 62 mph.</p><p>The strong winds originated from a low pressure system moving toward Lake Michigan from Madison, Wisconsin, where northern Illinois and northwest Indiana are on the southern end of the system, according to Brett Borchardt, a meteorologist with the National Weather Service</p><p>"It's sort of the same as a severe thunderstorm," Borchardt said. "It's a wind strong enough to knock down tree limbs, make driving very difficult and even lead to some power outages."</p><h3>‘This is what happens in the spring’</h3><p>By Friday night, ComEd was reporting about 1,200 outages. That's down from more than 30,000 outages earlier in the day, largely centered on the North Side and in the northwest suburbs, according to <a class="Link" href="https://www.comed.com/outages/experiencing-an-outage/outage-map" target="_blank" >the company’s outage map.</a></p><p>A spokesperson for ComEd told the Chicago Sun-Times that emergency crews, which helped restore power to customers during severe storms earlier this week, were already in the area and providing assistance. Power was restored to 47,000 customers Friday, according to ComEd. </p><p>ComEd said customers are being affected "for a number of reasons" related to the wind, from electrical poles being knocked down to tree branches blowing into power lines. The utility company didn't offer a timeline for when all customers will have their power restored. </p><p>"It's not like a thunderstorm, where it passes by. These are ongoing winds through the day," the ComEd spokesperson said. "We're just going to keep banging away and getting people restored as quickly as we can." </p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item"> <bsp-carousel class="Carousel aria-label="Next"> <svg class="FlickityCustomNavigation-button-icon"><use xlink:href="#icon-right"></use></svg> </button> </div> </div></div> </bsp-carousel> </div> </div><p>Lincoln Park resident Paul Fowler's power went out around midnight and again around 8:15 a.m. before it was restored two hours later. </p><p>He and several other residents living on Diversey Parkway and Bosworth Avenue said the power rarely goes out in their neighborhood but that the variety in weather isn’t uncommon for Chicago. </p><p>"This is what happens in the spring. There's weather fluctuations. It's cold, it's warm, there's wind," said Fowler, 61. "That's Chicago." </p><p>Borchardt said Saturday looks to be a "break in the action" before another storm system approaches Sunday night. </p><p>The system could bring severe thunderstorms and snowfall, and temperatures are expected to plummet from the 40s to the 20s Monday and Tuesday, according to the weather service. The wind chill could struggle to get above minus 5 degrees by early next week. </p><p>"That's gonna be a storm system that could give us a little bit of everything," Borchardt said. </p>
entries.author: Mohammad Samra

entries.title: Logan Square man found guilty of killing girlfriend
entries.summary: <p>A Logan Square man was convicted by a jury Thursday of killing his girlfriend and hiding her dismembered body inside his apartment nearly three years ago.</p><p>Genesis Silva, 36, was found guilty of murder in the June 2023 fatal stabbing of Brittany Battaglia, 33.</p><p>Battaglia’s body was found nearly decapitated in a large duffel bag in Silva’s apartment in the 2000 block of North Kimball Avenue.</p><p>Days before her body was found, Battaglia told her roommate that she was going to the home of Silva, whom she had been dating for more than a year, prosecutors said.</p><p>Battaglia was supposed to attend a party the same night but never showed up, and she was reported missing after her family could not reach her.</p><p>Detectives made a traffic stop on Silva while investigating her disappearance. He returned to his home with detectives, where they found the large duffel bag in his bedroom, prosecutors said. Silva was told to stay outside and was taken into custody when he tried sneaking back inside through a back stairway.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" </div><p>Battaglia’s death was ruled a homicide by multiple incised wounds, according to the Cook County medical examiner’s office.</p><p>A machete was found in Silva’s backpack and a second machete was discovered in his car, prosecutors said. Detectives also found two cell phones, two small pocketknives and an ice pick in Silva’s possession.</p><p>Silva was initially charged only with concealment of a homicidal death. He was charged with first-degree murder about six months later.</p><p>A hearing on a post-trial motion is set for April 9. Silva’s sentencing hearing hasn’t been scheduled.</p>
entries.author: Kade Heather

entries.title: Marist speeds past York into the 4A state championship game
entries.summary: <p>CHAMPAIGN—Marist is built to run. Charles Barnes, Stephen Brown and Kendall Meyers are long and athletic. TJ Tate and Adoni Vassilakis are speedy ballhandlers who make quick decisions.</p><p>York wanted to stop all that on <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/high-school-basketball/scores/2026/03/09/ihsa-boys-basketball-state-finals-scores-illinois" >Friday</a> in the Class 4A semifinals at State Farm Center. The Dukes are the Cinderella team at the IHSA state finals. They used strength and size and a punishing zone defense as their weapon to pull off several upsets the past few weeks.</p><p>The RedHawks didn’t let that happen, overwhelming York in the first quarter on the way to a 55-38 victory. Marist will face conference foe Benet in the 4A state championship game on Saturday.</p><p>Myers had five steals and played a large part in the first quarter when the RedHawks forced York into nine turnovers. The Dukes had 17 turnovers in the game.</p><p>“They exploited our weaknesses,” York coach Mike Dunn said. “We sometimes struggle with some pressure and turned the ball over in the first quarter and just never recovered from that.”</p><p>The game was even after the first quarter, but the damage was done.</p><p>“That was our game plan,” Marist coach Brian Hynes said. “They were bigger than us, but we had better athletes to get up and pressure them.”</p><p>Meyers led the RedHawks (32-5) with 15 points and Barnes scored 14.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-340000" name="image-340000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/56f62dd/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3220x1807+0+0/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F59%2F64%2F135cc34540a49ddc6948f41bf7d6%2Fob-cst-031426-105.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/97d1ada/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3220x1807+0+0/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F59%2F64%2F135cc34540a49ddc6948f41bf7d6%2Fob-cst-031426-105.jpg data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Marist’s Adoni Vassilakis (0) celebrates with Kendall Meyers (20) during the 4A semifinal game against York at State Farm Center.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Kirsten Stickney/For the Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>“Before the season started [Myers] had Division I [scholarship] offers and I had to tell him he would be the sixth man,” Hynes said. “It is that type of character that showed on the court tonight. Relentless effort and being a family.”</p><p>Marist assisted on 12 of its 21 field goals and had 13 steals. The larger college court seems tailor-made for the RedHawks.</p><p>“It definitely helped us deal with their zone, to space them out,” Barnes said. “They are bigger but a little slower than us.”</p><p>Hunter Stepanich led York (32-5) with 10 points and seven rebounds. The Princeton football recruit had a phenomenal high school career. He also played in a state football and a state volleyball championship game.</p><p>“I’m so proud of these guys,” Dunn said. To get to where we are at at 32-5 with no expectations at the beginning of the season. They just kept getting better and better.</p><p>“Obviously I’m disappointed by the outcome and the performance. But it doesn’t take away from what these kids have done.”</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/high-school-basketball/2026/02/17/benet-basketball-marist" >Marist lost to Benet 77-68</a> in Chicago on Feb. 17. This is the first trip to state for the RedHawks and will be their first title game appearance.</p><p>“I don’t think it has really hit me,” Hynes said. “The last couple of weeks have been unbelievable. I see these kids every day so I am not surprised. It is great for Marist, great for our community. There has been a lot of talk that [the Benet vs. DePaul Prep semifinal] is the state championship. That got us pretty fired up.”</p>
entries.author: Michael O'Brien

entries.title: With Big Dance next, is Illinois a fatally flawed team? Has to be asked after déjà vu loss to Wisconsin
entries.summary: <p>Is no lead safe against Wisconsin? Or is <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/college-sports" target="_blank" >Illinois</a> just not a team of closers?</p><p>Are overtime games the Illini’s kryptonite? Or is the whole point that they should be avoiding so many trips to OT in the first place?</p><p>Are <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/college-sports/2026/03/12/big-tens-best-michigan-nebraska-michigan-state-illinois-enter-postseason-chat-on-friday" target="_blank" >these Illini</a> — high on talent, often great offensively — simply inconsistent on defense or fatally flawed at that end of the court? And are they playing as hard as a team must, especially in March?</p><p>There are serious questions about a 24-8 team coach Brad Underwood still calls “one of the best in the country” as it heads to the NCAA Tournament after a 91-88 loss to Wisconsin in the Big Ten tournament quarterfinals, the Illini’s fifth loss — and fourth in OT — in their last nine games.</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/college-sports/2026/03/12/big-tens-best-michigan-nebraska-michigan-state-illinois-enter-postseason-chat-on-friday" >Big Ten’s big boppers — Michigan, Nebraska, Michigan State and Illinois — enter the postseason chat on Friday</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/college-sports/2026/03/08/big-ten-tournament-illinois-michigan-nebraska-purdue-united-center-chicago" target="_blank" >Big Ten tournament preview: An Illinois-Michigan rematch Saturday at the United Center would be huge</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p>“Sometimes, when we make shots, we get casual on the other end and we think it’s just easy,” Underwood said. “I had that feeling today.”</p><p>Déjà vu, one would think the feeling was. That’s because essentially the same game had already taken place Feb. 10 in Champaign, when the Illini led by 12 with eight minutes to go but let the Badgers storm back and win 92-90 in OT, with guards Nick Boyd and John Blackwell combining for 49 points. This time, the lead was a cushy 15 at 60-45 nearing the midpoint of the second half before Boyd and Blackwell — who went wild with 38 and 31 points, respectively — took full advantage of a team with its foot off the gas pedal.</p><p>Another double-digit lead against the Badgers. Another total fade-out by the Illini. You almost couldn’t make it up, except you don’t have to because there appears to be a script.</p><p>“It just looked like they wanted it more, which cannot happen in these types of games,” center Tomislav Ivisic said. “When you get into OT and you’ve played bad the last 10 minutes, it’s hard to just change that. It doesn’t work like that. You’ve got to be locked in for 40 minutes. We can’t choose which possession we want to play hard, which one we don’t want to play hard.”</p><p>The 7-1 Ivisic and 6-9 freshman forward David Mirkovic definitely weren’t playing hard enough when neither got a body on Blackwell and let the 6-4 guard steal an offensive rebound right in between them, leading to a pair of free throws that tied the game with under a minute left in regulation. How do the Illini’s top two rebounders let that happen at such a crucial time?</p><p>Senior guard Kylan Boswell, reputedly the team’s top defender, hurt the cause even more so in the game’s very first minute. After forcing Boyd into a turnover on the game’s first possession, Boswell got into Boyd’s face and drew a technical.</p><p>“I was [just] making sure he understood that I was out the last game,” said Boswell, who was injured and missed the first go-round between the teams.</p><p>Right after getting teed up, Boswell was whistled for a loose-ball foul at the other end and had to report to the bench. So much for getting the payback he’d been looking for after watching Boyd and Blackwell celebrate on the Illini’s home turf.</p><p>“I was pissed after that game seeing them two run up and down the court,” Boswell said, “and then today I got two fouls in the first minute of the game and now I can’t play again. I’m very frustrated with that.”</p><p>It has been a real problem, containing athletic guards who can score. The Big Dance is going to have a good many of them, no doubt.</p><p>“Is it an issue?” Boswell said. “It’s the Big Ten. The best guards in the nation play in this league.”</p><p>Boyd loved the early interaction with Boswell — even though he picked up a tech, too — and he and Blackwell did the same run-around thing after the rematch, waving and popping their red jerseys at the United Center crowd. Had to be a heck of a lot of fun for the Badgers (24-9) to complete another comeback like that and move on to a semifinal date with top-seeded Michigan.</p><p>“Just the way we’re built,” Boyd said. “We could be down 20 and we can still come back.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" > <div class="TweetUrl"> <blockquote class="twitter-tweet"><p lang="en" dir="ltr">Nick Boyd on his altercation with Kylan Boswell at the beginning of the game.<br><br>"I needed that."<br><br>Boyd set a <a href="https://twitter.com/hashtag/Badgers?src=hash&ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw">#Badgers</a> Big Ten Tournament record 38 points to go along with 6 assists, 5 rebounds, and 3 steals. <a href="https://t.co/hAbv2e9a7Q">pic.twitter.com/hAbv2e9a7Q</a></p>— Evan Flood (@Evan_Flood) <a href="https://twitter.com/Evan_Flood/status/2032579023532970363?ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw">March 13, 2026</a></blockquote> <script async src="https://platform.twitter.com/widgets.js" charset="utf-8"></script> </div> </div> </div></div><p>Boyd scored a quick seven after Boswell went out, and the Badgers led 10-5. But the Illini hammered back with a 23-3 run, with Boswell, back in the game, hitting a pull-up three to make it 28-13.</p><p>The Illini coughed up almost all of that lead before halftime. Then they went up 15 again in the second half and blew that lead, too. Again, how does this team let that happen?</p><p>“We got complacent,” Underwood said.</p><p>Going through all that just to lose in OT again doesn’t seem like much of a plan.</p><p>“Is this a story if you get beat by 20?” Underwood said. “It’s because they’re one-possession games.”</p><p>There tend to be a lot of those in the Big Dance, too.</p><p>Last year, the Illini faded down the stretch, and it was no surprise when they were bounced from the Dance in the second round. Boswell and Ivisic were key players on that team, and each insists this team is far better off as of mid-March.</p><p>“We’re more mature and way more connected,” Boswell said.</p><p>“This team’s better, definitely, and has better potential,” Ivisic said. “But we’ve got to learn to play for 40 minutes.”</p><p>Isn’t it kind of too late to learn that?</p><p>“I don’t know,” Ivisic said. “We’ll see.”</p>
entries.author: Steve Greenberg

entries.title: CPS Board names 3 finalists to lead district: Interim CEO King, former NYC, East Baton Rouge schools chiefs
entries.summary: <p>The Chicago Board of Education announced three finalists to run the school district Friday.</p><p>One surprising name on the list: Macquline King, who has been serving as interim leader of Chicago Public Schools since June, after originally being told she was not in the running for the permanent position.</p><p>She joins Meisha Porter, the first Black woman to lead New York City’s school system, and Sito Narcisse, the son of Haitian immigrants who led Louisiana’s second-largest school district and oversaw secondary schools in the District of Columbia.</p><p>Next week, the finalists will meet with Mayor Brandon Johnson and will be interviewed by a 15-member panel that includes students, parents, teachers and community members.</p><p>It’s unclear what role the mayor will play in picking the school district’s permanent leader, but the school board says on its website that the mayor will be part of the “decision making process” and that they will work “closely together to find a leader that can champion our district into its next chapter.”</p><p>The Chicago Teachers Union, which is allied with Johnson, believes that until there’s a fully elected board, state law gives the mayor authority to appoint CPS’ leader. CTU Vice President Jackson Potter argued that’s a good thing, given Johnson’s teaching experience.</p><p>“For so long, we've had these people chosen by the Broad Foundation and billionaires, like the Waltons and the Bill & Melinda Gates Foundation, people who were more responsible for financial oversight, such as Pedro Martinez, than learning and instruction,” Potter said. “So having someone who understands the education world granularly is really an advantage.”</p><p>Norma Rios-Sierra, a board member who represents the North Side, said the finalists were leaders who had all worked in large cities, like Chicago.</p><p>“We wanted someone who understood the city and understood the challenges and could really jump in and do the work,” she said. She said she is looking forward to having someone in place who can create the type of stability CPS needs.</p><p>The announcement comes after a <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/education/chicago-school-board/2026/02/13/is-cps-superintendent-search-on-track-or-stalled-depends-who-you-ask-on-school-board" target="_blank" ><u>months-long search process</u></a> that encountered several hiccups.</p><p>Originally, school board members <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/education/chicago-school-board/2025/11/07/cps-school-board-signs-ndas-faces-criticism-about-transparency-in-ceo-search" target="_blank" ><u>did not plan to make the names of the finalists public</u></a> because the search firm they hired said that would discourage candidates from applying. In November, <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/education/2025/11/07/school-leaders-from-new-york-city-and-denver-finalists-cps-top-job-ceo-superintendent" target="_blank" ><u>Porter and another candidate’s name became public</u></a>, and that candidate dropped out of contention.</p><p>At that point, the search was re-opened and board members conducted a new round of interviews.</p><h3>King makes a comeback as a candidate</h3><p>The fact that King was not a finalist in November <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/education/2025/11/05/cps-ceo-search-macquline-king" target="_blank" ><u>became a point of contention</u></a>. The West Side NAACP and some school board members suggested that she be kept on until at least January 2027 — when the board will go from being partially appointed by the mayor to being fully elected by voters.</p><p>King, a former CPS principal who previously served in the mayor’s office, had irked some board members during last summer's contentious budget discussions. Though the mayor’s appointees put her in the role, she presented a budget that did not include borrowing, which the mayor’s office wanted to shore up any potential shortfalls.</p><p>This past fall she had the challenging task of figuring out how the school district should address the aggressive immigration enforcement that sent waves of fear through communities. Though some community activists have complained that the school district was slow to respond, CPS eventually stood up a 24-hour command center to help schools.</p><p>At one board meeting, King became emotional as she talked about having to support families who were directly impacted by immigration enforcement.</p><p>School board member Jitu Brown, who represents a West Side district, said the last few months have been an “on the job interview” for King. He points to the way she has handled the closings of privately managed public schools. He said she has been attentive to parents and teachers at The Chicago Academy for the Arts, which the district is taking over, and Aspira Charter Schools, which is abruptly closing two high schools mid-year.</p><p>“You just saw her dealing with crisis situations, being responsive in a way that was different,” he said.</p><h3>Notable absence of Latino candidates in finalist pool</h3><p>Almost half of CPS students are Latino and many community members wanted to see a Latino candidate for CEO. But there are none among the finalists.</p><p>Chalkbeat Chicago reported that a fourth finalist dropped out. Board members would not say whether that person was Latino. They tell WBEZ and the Sun-Times that they were focused on finding someone who represented their values.</p><p>When former CEO Pedro Martinez and Mayor Brandon Johnson clashed in 2024 — ultimately leading to Martinez's ouster — Latino organizations, elected officials and civic leaders <a class="Link" href="https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=RkxhTMjJE3Y" target="_blank" ><u>came to Martinez’s defense</u></a>. Among them was Juan Rangel, a former charter school operator who is now CEO of The Urban Center, a nonprofit that advocates for quality schools.</p><p>On Friday, Rangel said it was “unfortunate” that no Latinos were among the final list of candidates and said it showed that the community continues to be an “afterthought” for CPS.</p><p>“It's about having leaders that look like the communities that they serve,” Rangel said. “Apparently not at CPS.”</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://alumni.mercy.edu/meisha-ross-porter" target="_blank" ><u>Porter was born and raised</u></a> in New York City and attended public schools there. She holds a degree in English from Hunter College, where she concentrated on cross-cultural literature and Black and Puerto Rican studies.</p><p>Porter worked for the New York City school system for more than 20 years, rising from teacher to principal to executive superintendent of schools in the Bronx, New York City’s largest borough. She was named the first Black woman chancellor of New York City schools in February 2021, but her term was short. She was replaced by newly elected Mayor Eric Adams in January 2022.</p><p>She did a short stint as the leader of a community foundation in New York and is currently a fellow for The Center for Educational Innovation, a nonprofit organization that focuses on “building high performing schools in underserved communities.”</p><p>Narcisse served as the superintendent of East Baton Rouge Parish schools in Louisiana from 2021 through 2024, according to his LinkedIn profile. He’s also held leadership roles in Metro Nashville Public Schools and Montgomery County Public Schools in Maryland.</p><p>He currently works with SJ Apple Group, a consulting agency that helps K-12 school districts develop “data-driven solutions that improve student outcomes.” The consulting group <a class="Link" href="https://www.sjapple.com/team" target="_blank" ><u>lists him as its founder</u></a>.</p><p>Narcisse grew up speaking Creole and French in New York. He went through the public school system there while learning English. He graduated from Kennesaw State University in Georgia with a degree in French and went on to earn a master’s of education from Vanderbilt University and a doctorate in education administration from the University of Pittsburgh.</p><p>In an interview with an <a class="Link" href="https://dmj.dmgroupk12.com/articles/taking-on-the-challenges-of-urban-education" target="_blank" ><u>education management group</u></a>, he said that to him, public education has “always been about impact, specifically on Black and Brown kids, because I’m a product of that growing up in New York.”</p>
entries.author: Emmanuel Camarillo

entries.title: Colin Stack's last-second free throw sends Benet to the 4A title game
entries.summary: <p>CHAMPAIGN—In basketball, just as in life, the most important decisions come down to trust. Who will you trust when the game is on the line?</p><p>Benet seniors Jayden Wright and Colin Stack have spent years developing a special connection on the court. It helped the Redwings to the Class 4A state championship last year and <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/high-school-basketball/scores/2026/03/09/ihsa-boys-basketball-state-finals-scores-illinois" >Friday</a> at State Farm Center it made all the difference in a 39-38 semifinal win against DePaul Prep.</p><p>The game was tied with 2 seconds left and Benet was inbounding the ball under the basket. Redwings coach Gene Heidkamp and DePaul Prep coach Tom Kleinschmidt called a combined five timeouts, changing plays every time.</p><p>“We changed the play 15 times,” Heidkamp said. “Their defense is so good. It is hard to get the ball near the rim with [Rashaun] Porter and [Gus] Johnson and their length.”</p><p>The final play came down to the trust that has grown between Wright and Stack. Wright threw the ball up where only a 7-1 athlete could grab it. Stack did and was fouled.</p><p>“I have a lot of trust in Colin so I just tried to put it up where he could get it, and he had a good look at it,” Wright said.</p><p>Then the hard part fell to Stack. Two chances at a game-winning free throw with 1.1 seconds left. He missed the first and made the second.</p><p>“[Teammate] Ryan Walsh said 'it is you in an empty gym,'” Stack said. “That just really went through my head. I missed the first one. Then I blocked it out and focused on the second one.”</p><p>Even the final play came down to trust. Heidkamp scrapped his plans and trusted his players.</p><p>“Ed [Stasys] came to me in the huddle and said let's run this little action,” Heidkamp said. “That is not a play we have. That was Ed and Colin making it up, and it worked about as well as it could. [Wright] made a great play, kept the ball high.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Benet’s Ed Stasys (23) shoots a three against DePaul Prep at State Farm Center.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Allen Cunningham/For the Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>DePaul Prep (32-4) had the best chance to win at the end. The Rams had the ball with a minute left, and the score tied at 38. They ran the clock down to ten seconds but then Benet junior Perry Tchiegne stole the ball.</p><p>“We made some mistakes and they got some baskets to tie it up,” Heidkamp said. “Then they had the ball to win it, and we had a huge defensive stop. That was the difference in the game.”Wright led Benet (36-1) with 11 points and Stasys, a 6-8 junior, finished with 10 points and 10 rebounds.</p><p>“We weren’t going to be able to score, and they weren’t going to be able to score,” Kleinschmidt said. “We knew that. Both sides have tough kids that are competitors.”</p><p>Benet won the rebounding battle 26-16. The Redwings also beat DePaul Prep in the Pontiac Holiday Tournament title game in late December.</p><p>Porter led the Rams with 14 points and Rykan Woo scored 11.</p><p>The loss ends DePaul’s run of three consecutive state titles. They won the last two Class 3A titles and the 2A title in 2023.</p><p>Benet will face conference rival Marist in the 4A title game at 6 p.m. on Saturday.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" > <div class="TweetUrl"> <blockquote class="twitter-tweet"><p lang="en" dir="ltr">Stack fouled. Heading to the line to try and win the game. <a href="https://t.co/pIeGCQUWMS">pic.twitter.com/pIeGCQUWMS</a></p>— Michael O'Brien (@michaelsobrien) <a href="https://twitter.com/michaelsobrien/status/2032595693668270573?ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw">March 13, 2026</a></blockquote> <script async src="https://platform.twitter.com/widgets.js" charset="utf-8"></script> </div> </div> </div></div><p></p>
entries.author: Michael O'Brien

entries.title: Bulls lose West Coast finale but get some progress on Jaden Ivey front
entries.summary: <p>LOS ANGELES – Jaden Ivey has his coach’s vote of confidence.</p><p>Does that mean the guard will be back with the Bulls and with Billy Donovan next season? Too early to answer that, especially since he will be a restricted free agent this summer. But in the talks Donovan has had with the medical team, he sounded optimistic that Ivey will return to the player he was coming out of Purdue and his first three seasons in Detroit before he fractured his leg.</p><p>“I think I have a pretty good feel for him as a player when he’s healthy,” Donovan said Friday. “He was really explosive, downhill, two-way player, can defend, really improved his shooting. The biggest thing I would want to know from our medical guys, and I have talked a little bit about this is, ‘OK, does a summer really bring his strength back?’ That would be my biggest thing.</p><p>“Our medical guys, when I’ve spoken to them, they feel really, really optimistic and positive that he will regain that strength, but strength doesn’t necessarily come back just like that. It’s going to take a period of time.”</p><p>Steps were taken in that direction as Ivey was assigned to the G League Windy City Bulls, got a workout with them back in Chicago, and could work out with the NBA roster on Sunday.</p><p>Considering how Friday against the Clippers worked out, the Bulls could use the help.</p><p>Playing in the finale of a five-game West Coast trip, the Bulls (27-40) looked like it, a step behind the home team most of the night in the 119-108 loss.</p><p>They did finish the trip 2-3, which wasn’t ideal for the tanking effort, but did give them some good moments moving beyond this season. Matas Buzelis had a solid series, including a career night (41 points) against the Warriors, and Josh Giddey registered his 11<sup>th</sup> triple-double of the season, finishing with 20 points, 11 rebounds and 10 assists.</p><p>As for Ivey, who was acquired in the Kevin Huerter trade, maybe he makes it back to help, but time isn’t on his side.</p><p>“I think a lot of it is going to be predicated on where his strength is at,” Donovan said of him possibly returning. “The improvement and growth he’s made in the strength area.”</p><p></p><p><b>Career mark</b></p><p></p><p>Giddey entered the finale of the West Coast trip shooting a career-best 38.7% from three-point range this season, after setting that mark last year with the Bulls ay 37.8%.</p><p>What’s been the change for Giddey?</p><p>“He’s really put a lot of time into it to his credit,” Donovan said. “I think my responsibility is to give him confidence on the ones he’s supposed to take and clearly he’s a guy that has done a terrific job of getting into the paint. He’s got to have the balance between sometimes guys are backing up to contain the dribble and those shots are there. I think he’s taking good ones. The more he can take that shot and he’s shooting it well, it’s going to open up his game.”</p><p></p><p><b>Medical tent</b></p><p></p><p>The Bulls did get Patrick Williams back on the floor against the Clippers, after he was dealing with left ankle soreness, but while one forward was back in, another went out.</p><p>Because Jalen Smith has been hampered with a calf strain, the Bulls opted to sit him in the back end of the back-to-back.</p>
entries.author: Joe Cowley

(This is your style. If you come across a hot tip or something useful, you like to pass it along.) Children might be an increased responsibility today. Romance will be serious.</p><h2>Capricorn (Dec. 22-Jan. 19)</h2><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-4d0000" name="html-embed-module-4d0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>An <b>average</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p>You're thrifty and practical (even though you often spend money on designer labels). Today, discussions about finances or possessions that relate to your family, or perhaps a parent or an older relative, might be a concern.</p><h2>Aquarius (Jan. 20-Feb. 18)</h2><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-7b0000" name="html-embed-module-7b0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>positive</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p>You have very definite ideas today about how to do things in a practical, sensible way, and you want others to listen to you. This could be related to old money issues. Whatever the case, you want to be heard. Fortunately, with Venus in your house of communications, you'll be charming and diplomatic!</p><h2>Pisces (Feb. 19-March 20)</h2><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-4d0000" name="html-embed-module-4d0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>An <b>average</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p>Research will go well today because you’re determined and persevering. You might want to know more about financial matters or something related to a possession you have. Keep digging because you’ll find what you’re looking for.</p><h2>If today is your birthday</h2><p>Theoretical physicist Albert Einstein (1879-1955) shares your birthday. You are perceptive, open-minded and astute. You can evaluate a situation and visualize possible scenarios. This is the last year of a nine-year cycle for you, which means it's time to take inventory of your life. Do some internal and external housecleaning. Get rid of whatever holds you back.</p>
entries.author: Georgia Nicols

entries.title: Deadly strike on school symbolizes U.S.-Israel 'aggression' for many Iranians
entries.summary: <p>I remember chicken schnitzel and near beer printed on restaurant menus alongside ghormeh sabzi and zereshk polo.</p><p>The ruins of <a class="Link" href="https://whc.unesco.org/en/list/114/" target="_blank" >Persepolis</a>, horse-drawn carriages and a desolate pair of <a class="Link" href="https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Boots_of_Reza_Shah_(8753483887).jpg" target="_blank" >large bronze riding boots</a> severed from its owner — a colossus statue of Reza Shah Pahlavi — also remain etched in my mind. </p><p>The exquisite silk rugs are the hardest to forget since the few my parents purchased ended up in my childhood home, where my mom has them unfurled whenever she wants to make an impression.</p><p>My vivid memories of Iran pale in comparison to <a class="Link" href="https://www.negarmortazavi.com/" target="_blank" >Negar Mortazavi’s</a> recollections of the country's resplendent architecture, ancient culture and often mischaracterized population.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-e40000" name="module-e40000"></a> <div class="RichTextSidebarModule-title">Columnist </div> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody">Columnist</div> </div><p>The Tehran native, who immigrated to the United States to study in Massachusetts a year after my family's unforgettable 2001 trip, immediately thought of her loved ones when U.S. and Israeli forces started bombing Iran a little over a week into Ramadan, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/nation-world/2026/02/28/iran-trump-israel" target="_blank" >assassinating</a> Iranian Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei.</p><p>Then, as more grim news of the initial strikes unfolded, she started having flashbacks to the 1980s, when she and her classmates rushed to take cover as sirens blared during the Iran-Iraq War. </p><p>"This could have been me," Mortazavi said of the <a class="Link" href="https://time.com/article/2026/03/11/iran-school-strike-minab-tomahawk/" target="_blank" >175 people, mostly children</a>, who were killed when a missile, likely belonging to the U.S., struck the Shajareh Tayyebeh elementary school in Minab, a rural city in Southern Iran known for its date palm groves and citrus trees.</p><p>The timing of the Feb. 28 attack — well before Iran started its retaliatory campaign on U.S. and Israeli military bases in the Gulf region — is just another example of how little the West and its key Middle East ally care about protecting civilians when they bomb Muslim-populations, Mortazavi said.</p><p>"Not all people matter," said Mortazavi, a senior fellow of the Washington, D.C.–based Center for International Policy and co-host of the "Iran Podcast."</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-floatRight> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-620000" name="image-620000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/c71f535/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6000x3367+0+316/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fc9%2F20%2F7e4952ec44feac7fa2f29e4719d7%2Faptopix-iran-us-israel.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/81e520b/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6000x3367+0+316/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fc9%2F20%2F7e4952ec44feac7fa2f29e4719d7%2Faptopix-iran-us-israel.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/1e59d1b/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6000x3367+0+316/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fc9%2F20%2F7e4952ec44feac7fa2f29e4719d7%2Faptopix-iran-us-israel.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Several rectangular cardboard boxes are on the floor. Inside the boxes are bodies of young children wrapped in white cloth." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/1e59d1b/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6000x3367+0+316/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fc9%2F20%2F7e4952ec44feac7fa2f29e4719d7%2Faptopix-iran-us-israel.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/643f76a/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6000x3367+0+316/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fc9%2F20%2F7e4952ec44feac7fa2f29e4719d7%2Faptopix-iran-us-israel.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/1e59d1b/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6000x3367+0+316/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fc9%2F20%2F7e4952ec44feac7fa2f29e4719d7%2Faptopix-iran-us-israel.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Coffins holding the bodies of mostly children killed in strike at the Shajareh Tayyebeh elementary school sit in a room in Minab, Iran. A preliminary U.S. military investigation has found that the U.S. was likely responsible for the Feb. 28 attack.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Amirhossein Khorgooei/AP </p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Mortazavi compared the deadly strike on the girls school, where some boys also studied, to the Vietnam War's My Lai Massacre.</p><p>At best, the strike on the school was a grave, negligent mistake: A preliminary U.S. military investigation recently revealed <a class="Link" href="https://apnews.com/article/iran-us-school-hegseth-trump-2ffff06808f7a584b0a03831897ab0b8" target="_blank" >outdated intelligence</a> likely led the U.S. to strike the two-story facility, which used to be housed on a military compound nearly a decade ago.</p> <p>Still, questions linger when you have a Defense secretary who <a class="Link" href="https://www.war.gov/News/News-Stories/Article/Article/4429836/hegseth-says-us-attacks-intensify-under-epic-fury-while-iranian-responses-slow/" target="_blank" >boasts</a> about a "mission delivered with overwhelming and unrelenting precision" and end up with a bombed-out school that is labeled on online maps and satellite imagery. </p><p>While <a class="Link" href="https://www.pbs.org/newshour/politics/fact-checking-trumps-claim-that-iran-has-tomahawk-missiles" target="_blank" >Donald Trump deflects</a> on the plausibility of U.S. involvement, many Iranians, including those vehemently opposed to their authoritarian, theocratic government, are mourning.</p><p>"The killings of schoolkids have been, and still are, and I think, will remain one of the main images of the current aggression on the country," <a class="Link" href="https://dohaforum.org/2023/speakers/hassan-ahmadian" target="_blank" >Hassan Ahmadian</a>, a political scientist who teaches at University of Tehran, told me.</p><p>The graphic pictures of the children killed in "cold blood by the U.S." have "stirred anger among the people like never before," inspiring many to "rally around the flag" against "indiscriminate bombing seen elsewhere in the country," he said.</p><p>A few days ago, The Tehran Times printed portraits of the youngest victims on its front page, demanding, "<a class="Link" href="https://x.com/TehranTimes79/status/2030737261365707114?s=20" target="_blank" >Trump, Look Them in the Eyes.”</a></p><p>That's a challenging feat when the president chooses not to see and perpetuates and facilitates more death and destruction.</p><p>Previous Republican and Democrat-led administrations have also used democracy, security and women's rights as a pretext to unleash violence in parts of the world they view through a jaundiced, one-dimensional lens.</p><p>The Iran war is no different. </p><p>"These attacks, the Israeli-U.S. attacks on Iran, are doing nothing to protect the Iranian protesters and those who have been at the forefront of fighting the government," Agnes Callamard, the secretary general of Amnesty International and a former United Nations special rapporteur, said in a recent interview with the news program "<a class="Link" href="https://www.democracynow.org/2026/3/12/agnes_callamard" target="_blank" >Democracy Now!</a>"</p><p>Among the most vocal Iranians who have taken to the streets are thousands of girls and women, who <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/columnists/2022/10/14/23402561/protests-iran-hijab-mahsa-amini-womens-right-feminism-muslim-islam-france-india-secularism" target="_blank" >ramped up their calls</a> for a regime change after 22-year-old Mahsa Amini suspiciously died in police custody following her arrest for wearing her hijab too loosely in 2022. </p><p>Shrinking violet is not a term I would use for the outspoken and educated women I encountered in Iran 25 years ago.</p><p>I probably wouldn't describe them that way today, either. </p><p>Iran has a high female literacy rate — 96.1%, according to a <a class="Link" href="https://docs.un.org/en/A/HRC/59/NGO/264#:~:text=National%20University%20Entrance%20Exam:%20In,the%202023%E2%80%932024%20academic%20year%20." target="_blank" >report</a> submitted to the U.N. General Assembly last summer. Seventy percent of the Islamic Republic's university graduates in science, technology, engineering and mathematics are women, the report also shows.</p><p>A plane ticket isn't required to absorb the complexities of Iran's history and its people.</p><p>There's always the option to crack open a book and learn, which is all the Shajareh Tayyebeh elementary school students were trying to do that Saturday morning before they were killed. </p><p><i>Rummana Hussain is a columnist and leads the opinion coverage at the Sun-Times.</i></p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-2f0000" name="module-2f0000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody">Send letters to <a class="Link" href="mailto:letters@suntimes.com" target="_blank" >letters@suntimes.com</a>. More about <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/submitting-opinion-and-letters" >how to submit here</a>.</div> </div><p></p>
entries.author: Rummana Hussain

entries.title: Dear Abby: Son now hides his drug habits in plain sight
entries.summary: <p><b>DEAR ABBY:</b> My 29-year-old son, "Earl," has an MBA. He started drinking and doing drugs at the age of 16. Earl is highly intelligent, and he successfully hid his addiction from me. When I realized its existence, just before he left for college, it was too late for me to do anything. I offered to send him to rehab, but he refused.</p><p>I am a single mom. I tried to tell his father, but he was of no help. Earl lives in a different state now but visits me every few months. I recently realized that every time Earl visits, he buys drugs before he leaves for the airport. He has them in his carry-on bag. I am afraid Earl may be selling them. He says he stopped doing marijuana, but I can smell it on him. I'm worried something will happen to him. What can I do? — <i>POWERLESS IN TEXAS</i></p><p><b>DEAR POWERLESS:</b> Your fears are well-grounded. I'm surprised that when Earl's carry-on bag is scanned at the airport, the drugs haven't been spotted. I'm also surprised that if he has the drugs on him (reeking of marijuana) he hasn't encountered a drug-sniffing canine, which would also lead to an arrest. You ask what you can do about this sorry situation; the answer is nothing. Your son is an adult who may have to accept the consequences for taking the risks he has.</p><p><b>DEAR ABBY:</b> I just passed my 10th anniversary at the hospital where I am employed. This occasion is normally celebrated at a monthly 30-person meeting of managers and directors with a cake, photo, certificate and a small gift. The meeting came, but there was no cake, no photo, no certificate, etc. Just before the meeting ended, my supervisor said, "Oh, by the way, this month is 'Janet's' 10th anniversary." Everyone clapped, and that was it. It was obvious that they had forgotten, and he only remembered at the last minute.</p><p>I went to the person who normally arranges these types of things and asked if they had forgotten. She stuttered and had a bunch of excuses — the CEO wasn't around to sign the certificate, the gift was hung up due to tariffs, etc. I said "OK" and went back to my office. NOW my boss wants to make it a big deal. He and the COO are going to take me to lunch, and a surprise party with cake is being planned that I'm not supposed to know about.</p><p>Abby, I just don't want any of it. It feels like overcompensation and an afterthought. It is water under the bridge at this point, and it's embarrassing to me for them to do it now, like I had to ask. This is going to be forced on me. How can I stay polite and yet firm about turning this all down? — <i>FORGOTTEN BIRTHDAY GIRL</i></p><p><b>DEAR GIRL:</b> OK. Someone dropped the ball; they screwed up, and it hurt your feelings. Your employers are trying to make it up to you because they like, respect and care about you. Salve your wounded ego and do not pout. This may be overcompensation on their part, but be a good sport and let them make the effort. You deserve it. To do otherwise could cause ill will, and you don't want to do that just because somebody made a mistake.</p><p><i>Dear Abby is written by Abigail Van Buren, also known as Jeanne Phillips, and was founded by her mother, Pauline Phillips. Contact Dear Abby at www.DearAbby.com or P.O. Box 69440, Los Angeles, CA 90069.</i></p><p>To receive a collection of Abby's most memorable — and most frequently requested — poems and essays, send your name and mailing address, plus check or money order for $8 (U.S. funds), to: Dear Abby — Keepers Booklet, P.O. Box 446, Kings Mills, OH 45034-0446. (Shipping and handling are included in the price.)</p>
entries.author: Abigail Van Buren

entries.title: How we photographed the final funeral service for Rev. Jesse Jackson
entries.summary: <p>This week’s top photo comes from Visual Journalist <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/candace-chambers" target="_blank" >Candace Dane Chambers</a>.</p><h3><b>What makes this photo important?</b></h3><p>I shot four homegoing events for the Rev. Jesse Jackson, two public viewings of him lying in repose and two formal services. Many evocative images came out of those two weeks. The public outpouring was in the thousands, and many people shared personal interactions they had with Rev. Jackson, which was a testament to his decades of community work.</p><p>The bereaved Jackson children held space for others' grief while managing their own — much of it on a national scale. Elected officials and public figures spoke passionately about Rev. Jackson’s impact on the social and political fabric of our nation. Many of their remarks were charged with indictments of the present and hopes for the future, which they ascribed to Rev. Jackson.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/politics/2026/03/07/mourners-honor-rev-jesse-jackson-at-final-funeral-service" >The final homegoing service March 7 at the Rainbow PUSH Coalition</a> headquarters was the most intimate remembrance. Rev. Jackson had purchased the building, previously used as a synagogue, in 1971. Since then, it’s been the site of many organizing meetings and community programs and or spiritual fellowship.</p><h3><b>How did you get this photo?</b></h3><p>This service was the end of a marathon of coverage. The day before, I’d spent 10 hours shooting and editing <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/politics/2026/03/06/rev-jesse-jackson-chicago-service-house-of-hope-pullman-neighborhood-barack-obama-jb-pritzker" >the public remembrance at House of Hope</a>. I was at Rainbow PUSH early to capture what I thought would just be the procession in and out of the building, as we were under the impression we would not be allowed to photograph inside. To my surprise, there was a change in plans and the press was allowed in the sanctuary. This was the first time I’d been in the space during the day, and I was moved by its beauty. The room was held up by ornate pillars and outfitted with stained glass windows bathing a warm yellow glow across the pews. I spent most of the service focused on the stage, shooting many speakers and performers, including the legendary Stevie Wonder. But as the service drew on, I zoomed out and focused on the environment. I moved stage right and focused on the windows, cranking my exposure down so that the packed house became a sea of silhouettes. The sharp contrast between the window light and the shadowy room created a dynamic image that encapsulated the gravity of the moment.</p><h3><b>Technical details:</b></h3><ul class="rte2-style-ul" style="margin-top:0;margin-bottom:0;padding-inline-start:48px;" id="rte-9460a7e0-1f16-11f1-ad7a-f35fc19f6d46"><li>Equipment: Canon EOS R5 with an RF 28-70mm f/2L lens</li><li>Focal length: 28mm</li><li>Aperture: f/3.5</li><li>Exposure: 1/250</li><li>ISO: 400</li></ul><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/politics/2026/03/06/rev-jesse-jackson-chicago-service-house-of-hope-pullman-neighborhood-barack-obama-jb-pritzker" >Chicago comes out in force to honor Rev.
